request: We want to notify the right people when a freelancer's mission is about to end.

problem.value: Avoid missed or late handling of mission endings (gaps in freelancer coverage, late final invoicing, unprepared client/manager).
problem.evidence: 'notify the right people when a mission is about to end' — implies a current gap where end dates are not proactively surfaced.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether tracked today via manual calendar checks, spreadsheet, or nothing at all.

success_metrics.value: No mission ends 'missed' by client/internal staff; timely renewal or offboarding decisions.
success_metrics.evidence: Implicit in the request's intent ('the right people' notified in time).

actors.value: Likely: client operational contact, internal account/mission manager, possibly the freelancer themself.
actors.evidence: 'the right people' left undefined — context notes Contact has a role (billing/HR/operational) driving routing.

business_objects.evidence: 'freelancer's mission' and 'about to end' directly name Mission end date as the trigger field.

business_rules.value: Lead time before end (e.g. 15/30 days) not specified; may vary by client/contract type.
business_rules.evidence: Domain note: business rules often vary by client/contract/country — unspecified here.

workflow.value: Likely a scheduled/recurring check comparing today's date to mission end date, firing at a threshold; end-of-mission is a named event in the platform.
workflow.evidence: Context: 'End-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications, final invoicing and validation.' Trigger mechanics (scheduled vs event-driven, sync/async) not specified.

integrations.value: Notification channel (email/in-app) and possible downstream effects (final invoicing, freelancer offboarding) not specified.
integrations.evidence: Context explicitly links end-of-mission to notifications, final invoicing and validation as chained effects — raised above default medium.

permissions.value: Who can configure lead time/routing, who can see mission end alerts, unspecified.
permissions.evidence: Permissions are 'forgotten until acceptance testing' per context recurring traps.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear if lead time and notification routing are global defaults or client-specific configuration.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'notification routing' listed explicitly as client-specific configurable item.

constraints.value: No deadline, budget, or regulatory driver stated beyond general GDPR on HR/freelancer data.
constraints.evidence: Not mentioned; inferred from platform-wide GDPR note.

edge_cases.value: Mission end date extended/changed, mission cancelled early, multiple contacts per client with no role assigned, freelancer with concurrent missions.
edge_cases.evidence: Context recurring trap: one-to-many (client→contacts) and idempotency ('don't create/notify twice') both apply here.

reporting.value: No stated need for audit trail or dashboard of notifications sent.
reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit language in the request.

risks.value: Risk of duplicate or missed notifications if end date changes after first alert; risk of notifying wrong contact role.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context traps on idempotency and role-based routing.

questions.q: Who exactly are 'the right people' for a mission-end alert — the client's operational contact, the internal account/mission manager, the freelancer themself, or several of these depending on role?
questions.why: High impact/high uncertainty: notification routing shapes the whole build (per-contact-role logic vs. simple internal alert) and is explicitly called out as a recurring trap.

questions.q: How far before the end date should the alert fire (e.g. 15/30 days), and does that lead time vary by client, contract type, or mission length?
questions.why: High impact/empty: threshold logic is the core rule of the feature and the context flags rules that vary by client/contract as a frequent, easily-missed source of rework.

questions.q: Should this run as a recurring scheduled check (e.g. nightly job scanning upcoming end dates), or be tied to a specific event — and if the mission's end date is later changed or the mission is cancelled, should a pending alert be cancelled/rescheduled automatically?
questions.why: High impact/high uncertainty: sync vs async and idempotency-on-change are named traps that change error handling and cost significantly.

questions.q: What should happen alongside the notification — is it just an alert (email/in-app), or should it also kick off downstream actions like final invoicing prep or freelancer offboarding review?
questions.why: High impact: context notes end-of-mission is a chained trigger for invoicing/validation, so scope may be larger than 'send an email.'

questions.q: Is the lead time and notification routing meant to be globally standard, or configurable per client (as approval circuits and routing usually are on this platform)?
questions.why: High impact: determines whether this is a hardcoded rule or a client-configurable setting, a foundational architecture choice.

summary.objective: Proactively alert the relevant people before a freelance mission ends, so no mission end is missed and downstream actions (renewal, offboarding, final invoicing) happen on time.
summary.scope: A scheduled or event-driven check on Mission end dates that fires a notification once a configurable lead-time threshold is reached, routed to the correct role(s) (client contact, internal manager, and/or freelancer), with safeguards against duplicate alerts if the end date changes or the mission is cancelled.
assumptions: Notification recipients likely include the client's operational contact and the internal mission/account manager, possibly the freelancer.
A lead-time threshold (e.g. 15/30 days before end) is needed but not yet defined.
The check is likely a scheduled/recurring job rather than a single real-time event.
Notification routing and lead time are probably configurable per client, consistent with the platform's existing configurability model.
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like 'send an email' but end-of-mission is a chained trigger in this platform (notifications + final invoicing + validation), and mission end dates can change or be cancelled after an alert is scheduled — without clear rules on lead time, recipient roles, and idempotency on date changes, the team risks building a fragile one-shot notifier that duplicates or misses alerts and stops short of the real downstream need.

problem.value: Avoid missed renewals/extensions and ensure smooth offboarding (final invoicing, access revocation, handover) by alerting stakeholders before a mission lapses.
problem.evidence: Request: 'notify the right people when a mission is about to end' — no stated pain point, but context flags end-of-mission as a trigger for final invoicing and validation.

current_process.evidence: Not mentioned — likely manual (calendar reminders/spreadsheet) today, unconfirmed.

success_metrics.evidence: Not stated. Likely 'no missed end-dates / no coverage gaps', to confirm.

actors.value: Likely candidates: freelancer's manager/account owner, client contact(s) (operational/HR/billing role), possibly the freelancer themselves. Exact set unconfirmed.
actors.evidence: Request says 'the right people' without naming them. Context: Client/Contact notification routing depends on contact ROLE, not just the client — major open question.

business_objects.value: Mission entity: linked to Freelancer and Client, has start_date and end_date. Notification needs an end-date field and a lead-time parameter.
business_objects.evidence: Explicit: 'mission is about to end' — object and key dates given by context.

business_rules.value: How many days before end should the alert fire? Does the lead time vary by client, contract type, or mission duration?
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'a business rule can vary by client, contract or country' — timing rule not specified at all.

workflow.value: Unclear if it's a single alert or recurring reminders until an action (renew/extend/end) is taken, and how mission-date changes (extension, early termination) affect already-scheduled notifications.
workflow.evidence: Context: end-of-mission is an event that can trigger notifications AND final invoicing/validation — sequencing and re-trigger logic unspecified.

integrations.value: Channel not specified: email, in-app notification, or both. No mention of an external system to notify.
integrations.evidence: Request only says 'notify' — context stresses 'notify' must specify who/when/channel.

permissions.value: Who can configure the lead-time/recipient rules, and who can see the notification (e.g., should client contacts see internal remarks)?
permissions.evidence: Context: permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing; approval/notification rules are shared/cross-cutting.

config_vs_custom.value: Should notification timing/recipients be a standard global rule, or configurable per client (client-specific routing)?
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'notification routing' is explicitly listed as client-specific configurability on this platform.

constraints.value: No deadline or regulatory driver named.
constraints.evidence: Nothing stated; freelance missions aren't flagged as regulatory-sensitive in context.

edge_cases.value: Mission extended/shortened after notification scheduled; mission cancelled; contact missing/incomplete; multiple freelancers ending same week; duplicate notifications.
edge_cases.evidence: Context traps: idempotency (don't notify twice), incomplete source data, one-to-many ripple effects.

reporting.value: Not requested; no compliance/audit driver named for this feature.
reporting.evidence: No mention of audit or traceability need — keep low unless raised.

risks.value: Risk of wrong/missing recipients causing missed renewals; risk of duplicate/spam notifications if trigger not idempotent.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context traps around notification triggers.

questions.q: Who exactly are 'the right people' — the freelancer's internal manager, one or more client contacts (and which role: operational, HR, billing), the freelancer themselves, or a combination that may differ by client?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: notification routing on this platform depends on contact ROLE, not just the client — guessing wrong means rebuilding the recipient logic and permissions.

questions.q: How far in advance should the notification fire (e.g. 30/15/7 days before end), and does that lead time vary by client, contract type, or mission length?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: an unspecified, possibly variable business rule directly shapes the scheduling engine and configurability needs.

questions.q: If a mission's end date is later extended, shortened, or the mission is cancelled, should pending notifications be automatically rescheduled/cancelled — and should this be a one-time alert or repeated reminders until someone acts?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: determines whether the trigger is a simple date-check or a stateful, re-triggerable workflow — a major blind spot on effort.

questions.q: Should the lead-time and recipient rules be a single global setting, or configurable per client (like other notification routing on this platform)?
questions.why: High impact x high uncertainty: platform convention makes notification routing client-specific by default — confirms scope of the config layer to build.

questions.q: Which channel(s) should the alert use — email, in-app notification, both — and is there an existing notification system/template to reuse?
questions.why: Medium-high value: 'notify' is unspecified on channel; affects integration effort and reuse of existing infrastructure.

summary.objective: Automatically alert the relevant stakeholders ahead of a freelance mission's end date, so renewals, extensions, offboarding and final invoicing can be handled without last-minute scrambling.
summary.scope: Likely involves: a scheduled check on Mission.end_date, a configurable lead-time rule, resolution of recipients (client contact by role and/or internal manager), a notification dispatch (email/in-app), and safeguards against duplicate or stale alerts when mission dates change.
assumptions: Recipients likely include the freelancer's internal manager and/or a client contact with an operational or HR role — exact set to confirm.
A single lead-time window (e.g. X days before end) is assumed, possibly configurable per client.
Notification is assumed to be sent once, via email and/or in-app — repeated reminders not assumed unless confirmed.
No regulatory or audit requirement is assumed for this specific feature.
summary.blind_spot: The 'right people' likely depend on the client contact's ROLE (billing vs operational vs HR), not just the client — and mission end-dates can change (extension/early termination/cancellation) after a notification is scheduled, requiring rescheduling logic and idempotency to avoid duplicate or stale alerts. Both are easy to underestimate if treated as a simple 'send email X days before date' feature.

problem.value: Avoid missions ending without a replacement, extension, or final invoicing/offboarding being arranged in time.
problem.evidence: "notify the right people when a mission is about to end" implies a lead-time need, not just an end-of-mission log.

actors.value: Likely recipients: internal manager/staffing owner, client operational contact, possibly the freelancer.
actors.evidence: Context: Client/Contact roles (billing, HR, operational) drive routing; Mission links Freelancer + Client.

business_objects.value: Mission (start/end date) exists; needs a lead-time threshold and recipient/config list not present today.
business_objects.evidence: Context: "Mission ... linked to Freelancer and Client, has a start and end."

business_rules.evidence: Not stated who exactly is 'right' nor whether it varies by client/contract/mission type.

workflow.value: Presumed: a scheduled check X days before mission end date fires a notification event, likely async/queued rather than a live user action.
workflow.evidence: Context: end-of-mission is an event; sync vs async trigger distinction is a named recurring trap.

permissions.value: Notification routing/config likely restricted to admin/manager; visibility of mission end date already role-based.
permissions.evidence: Context: permission rules shared across modules; notification routing depends on contact role.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: notification routing and business rules are explicitly client-specific configurability areas.

edge_cases.evidence: Context traps: mission extended/cancelled after notification scheduled, duplicate notifications (idempotency), missing/incomplete end date.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit driver named for this specific feature.

questions.q: Who exactly counts as 'the right people' — the internal manager/staffing lead, the client's operational contact (vs billing/HR), the freelancer, or a combination — and does this vary by client or contract?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: this determines the whole notification-routing design and whether it must be configurable per client.

questions.q: How far before the end date should the notification fire (e.g. 30/15/7 days), is it a one-time alert or repeated reminders, and should this lead time be configurable per client/contract?
questions.why: uncertainty (inferred, low completeness) x high impact: defines the trigger mechanism and whether it's hardcoded or a config parameter — different builds.

questions.q: Should the lead-time/recipient rule be the same for every client, or configurable per client/contract like other approval/notification rules on this platform?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: platform defaults to configurable routing rules; hardcoding vs configuring is a major cost difference.

questions.q: What happens if the mission is extended, cancelled, or its end date changed after a notification was already scheduled or sent — should it cancel/reschedule, and how do we avoid duplicate notifications?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x high impact: idempotency and stale-trigger handling are named recurring traps for event-driven features like this.

questions.q: Which channel should be used (email, in-platform notification, both), and should the check run as a scheduled/async job or synchronously on some user action?
questions.why: uncertainty (empty) x medium-high impact: sync vs async changes error handling, UX and infra cost significantly.

summary.objective: Automatically alert the relevant stakeholders ahead of a freelance mission's end date, so replacement, extension, or offboarding/final invoicing can be arranged in time rather than reactively after the mission ends.
summary.scope: Add a lead-time trigger on the Mission entity: a scheduled (likely async) check comparing today's date to the mission end date, firing a notification to a configurable set of recipients (internal manager, client operational contact, freelancer) through a chosen channel (email/in-app), with safeguards against duplicate or stale notifications when a mission's end date changes.
assumptions: Recipients likely include the internal staffing manager and the client's operational contact, not just billing/HR contacts.
The trigger is a scheduled/asynchronous job rather than a synchronous check on user action.
The lead-time window and recipient list should be configurable per client/contract, consistent with the platform's existing configurability model for notification routing.
The rule must handle mission end-date changes (extension/cancellation) without producing duplicate or orphaned notifications.
summary.blind_spot: The request sounds like a simple reminder, but 'the right people' and 'about to end' are both undefined: who receives it likely varies by client/contract (contact role: operational vs billing), and the trigger must survive mission end-date changes without double-firing or missing the window — none of which is a trivial add-a-notification task.
